title: Timepoint 3 — Post-transplant
scenarioBrief: Her radiologist agreed to stop infusing FFP, and a PleurX catheter was placed. She then improved markedly and no longer needed additional drainage. She ultimately received a liver transplant (7/19/2027) and is now about two months out, doing well, with resolved ascites and hydrothorax and normalized liver function.

chartGate._notes: TP3 visibility: the complete record, including the transplant evaluation/listing note, the PleurX placement, the transplant hospitalization summary, and the post-transplant clinic note + labs/imaging showing resolution of ascites/hydrothorax and normalized synthetic function.

prompts.question: After her transplant, her need for paracentesis and thoracentesis dropped away entirely. What are the possible reasons for this adjustment in her drainage needs?

essential: The liver transplant RESOLVED the portal hypertension that drove the ascites and hepatic hydrothorax — so the fluid stopped re-accumulating and drainage was no longer needed
Restored hepatic synthetic function (normalized albumin) corrected the hypoalbuminemia that contributed to fluid accumulation
bonus: Frames it as the transplant correcting the ROOT CAUSE (cirrhosis/portal hypertension) rather than the bridges (diuretics, drainage, PleurX) treating the symptom
Notes corroborating normalization of INR, bilirubin, and platelets as evidence of restored liver function / regressing portal hypertension
Notes resolution of the hepatic hydrothorax mechanism (no ascitic fluid to track across the diaphragm)
redFlags: Attributing the improvement to the PleurX or diuretics rather than the transplant correcting the underlying physiology
Failing to connect the resolution to reversal of portal hypertension

scoringRubric.rubricText:
Total 3 points. Three of the five of the following (1 each up to 3):
- Reduction in total volume of blood products
- Changed alcohol intake
- Improved adherence to diuretics
- Improved nutrition
- Less salt in diet
APPLICABLE MAXIMUM: 3.
